blocks.cap: One address, a fistful of names. The green point sits three quarters of the way from \(0\) to \(1\). Cut the trip into \(4\) equal ticks and it lands on the 3rd, into \(8\) ticks and it lands on the 6th, into \(12\) ticks and it lands on the 9th, so \(\tfrac{3}{4}\), \(\tfrac{6}{8}\), and \(\tfrac{9}{12}\) are all the very same spot. You can multiply the top and bottom by anything to mint a new name for it, like \(\tfrac{15}{20}\). Out of all of them, only \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) is the lowest terms name tag.

blocks.x: A music app stores a drum rhythm internally as the fraction \(\tfrac{2}{5}\), but the little label on screen is tiny, so it will only show a name whose bottom number is at most \(40\). Anything with a bigger bottom overflows and gets cut off. Every name the app is allowed to use comes from multiplying the top and bottom of \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) by the same whole number \(k\), which gives \(\tfrac{2 \times k}{5 \times k}\). So the bottom of each name is \(5k\). The plain fraction \(\tfrac{2}{5}\) itself counts as the \(k = 1\) case. How many different names for this rhythm can the app display?
hints: Forget the top number for a moment. The only thing that decides whether a name fits is its bottom, and the bottom is always \(5k\). So you are really asking how many whole numbers \(k\) keep \(5k\) from going past \(40\). That is just counting multiples of \(5\), the kind of thing you did back in Chapter 3.
List the bottoms in order. With \(k = 1\) the bottom is \(5\), with \(k = 2\) it is \(10\), and so on. Keep going as long as the bottom stays at or below \(40\), then count how many you wrote down.
blocks.sol: Each allowed name is \(\tfrac{2k}{5k}\) for some whole number \(k\), and the rule is that the bottom \(5k\) must be at most \(40\). The top never causes trouble, so the whole question collapses into a single condition on the bottom, \(5k \le 40\). That is exactly a count of the multiples of \(5\) that land at or below \(40\), the move you learned in Chapter 3. Dividing the bound by the step gives \(40 \div 5 = 8\), so \(k\) can be \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\) and no further, since \(k = 9\) would push the bottom to \(45\) and overflow the label. Those eight values of \(k\) produce the bottoms \(5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40\), one legal name apiece. The neat idea here is that the whole family of names for a fraction is nothing more than a list of multiples, so counting names is counting multiples. There are \(\boxed{8}\) names.

blocks.x: Up to now we have worked on one fraction at a time, re-cutting it into bigger or smaller pieces while it stayed parked on the same point. The real test arrives when two fractions show up side by side and we ask a blunt question. Do these two name the same point, or not? One honest way to settle it is to reduce each fraction all the way down to its simplest name tag and see whether the tags match. That works every time, but there is a quicker road, and it grows straight out of re-cutting. If we re-cut both fractions until their pieces are the same size, meaning they share the same bottom number, then asking whether they match becomes nothing more than comparing two counts of identical pieces. Forcing a shared bottom like that is also the exact move we will lean on soon when we start adding fractions, so it pays to meet it now. Let us build the shared bottom first and let the shortcut fall out on its own.

Two ramps climb to the same loading dock. The east ramp rises \(5\) units of height over \(6\) units of run, so its slope is \(\frac{5}{6}\). The west ramp rises \(7\) units over \(8\) units of run, so its slope is \(\frac{7}{8}\). The steeper ramp is the one with the larger slope, but \(\frac{5}{6}\) and \(\frac{7}{8}\) have different bottoms, so you cannot tell which top is bigger just by looking at \(5\) and \(7\). A clean shared bottom is \(24\), since \(6\times 4 = 24\) and \(8\times 3 = 24\). Rebuild both slopes so they sit over a bottom of \(24\), and the contest turns into plain counting. Write the slope of the steeper ramp as the original fraction \(\frac{a}{b}\).
hints: You cannot compare the tops while the bottoms disagree. Force both fractions onto the same bottom of \(24\) first, then the bigger top wins. Multiplying top and bottom by the same number does not change a fraction's value, it just renames it.

Multiply \(\frac{5}{6}\) by \(\frac{4}{4}\) to get \(\frac{20}{24}\), and multiply \(\frac{7}{8}\) by \(\frac{3}{3}\) to get \(\frac{21}{24}\). Now both bottoms are \(24\), so compare the tops \(20\) and \(21\). The bigger top names the steeper ramp, and your answer should be its original unbuilt fraction.
blocks.sol: While the two slopes wear different bottoms, the numbers on top live on different scales, so \(5\) and \(7\) cannot be raced against each other yet. The fix is to give them a shared bottom. A bottom of \(24\) works for both, since \(6\times 4 = 24\) and \(8\times 3 = 24\). Renaming a fraction by multiplying its top and bottom by the same number keeps its value untouched, it only changes the costume. Build the east ramp up by \(\frac{4}{4}\): $$\frac{5}{6} = \frac{5\times 4}{6\times 4} = \frac{20}{24}.$$ Build the west ramp up by \(\frac{3}{3}\): $$\frac{7}{8} = \frac{7\times 3}{8\times 3} = \frac{21}{24}.$$ Now both ramps measure their rise in twenty-fourths, so the comparison is just counting. We have \(20\) twenty-fourths against \(21\) twenty-fourths, and \(21 > 20\), so \(\frac{21}{24}\) is the larger slope. That bigger slope is the renamed west ramp, whose original fraction is \(\frac{7}{8}\). The steeper ramp is the west ramp, with slope \(\boxed{7/8}\).

blocks.x: A baking judge has to settle an argument. One cook says her syrup, made with \(4\) scoops of sugar to \(6\) cups of water, is exactly as sweet as another batch made with \(6\) scoops of sugar to \(9\) cups of water. So the question is whether \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) are the same number. The judge could reduce both and compare, but he wants a test that never needs reducing, so he clears the bars instead. Both \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) are ordinary numbers, and two equal numbers stay equal when you multiply each by the same thing. He multiplies both sides by \(6\times 9\), the product of the two bottoms. On the left the \(6\) cancels and leaves \(4\times 9\). On the right the \(9\) cancels and leaves \(6\times 6\). So the two batches are equally sweet exactly when \(4\times 9\) equals \(6\times 6\). Compute both products. If they are equal, enter that common value. If they are not equal, enter \(0\).
hints: Multiplying \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) by \(6\times 9\) cancels the bottom \(6\) and leaves \(4\times 9\). Multiplying \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) by the same \(6\times 9\) cancels the bottom \(9\) and leaves \(6\times 6\). Now you only have to compare two whole-number products, no fractions left.
Work out \(4\times 9\) and \(6\times 6\) separately. If they land on the same number, that number is your answer. If they differ, the answer is \(0\).
blocks.sol: Follow the judge's move and clear the bars. He multiplies the two equal-or-not numbers \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) by the same thing, \(6\times 9\), which keeps any true equality true. On the left, \(\tfrac{4}{6}\times 6\times 9\) cancels the \(6\) on the bottom and leaves \(4\times 9\). On the right, \(\tfrac{6}{9}\times 6\times 9\) cancels the \(9\) on the bottom and leaves \(6\times 6\). Notice what survived. The top of the first fraction got paired with the bottom of the second, and the bottom of the first got paired with the top of the second. The fractions are equal exactly when these two crossed products match. Now just compute. \(4\times 9 = 36\) and \(6\times 6 = 36\). They agree, so \(\tfrac{4}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) really are the same number, and the common value is \(36\). That crossing trick is no accident. For any \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) and \(\tfrac{c}{d}\), clearing the bars the same way leaves \(a\times d\) against \(b\times c\), so the fractions are equal exactly when \(a\times d = b\times c\). The judge found his reduce-free test, and the answer here is \(\boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: If I want to know whether \(\frac{a}{b}\) and \(\frac{c}{d}\) are the same number, I do not have to reduce anything. I can re-cut both to the shared bottom \(b\times d\), where the first turns into \(a\times d\) of those tiny pieces and the second turns into \(c\times b\) of them, and since the pieces are now identical in size, the two fractions match exactly when those two counts match. So I just cross-multiply, top of each times the bottom of the other, and check whether \(a\times d\) equals \(b\times c\). And if they are not equal, the bigger product belongs to the bigger fraction, which means cross-multiplying also tells me which name points further along the number line.

Here is a small thing that trips people up. To reduce a fraction sounds like you are making the number smaller, but you never are. The fraction \(\frac{84}{120}\) and its reduced form \(\frac{7}{10}\) sit on the exact same point of the number line. What shrinks is only the size of the counts you write down, not the amount itself. An older name you sometimes see for a fraction in lowest terms is a fraction in its least terms, and clerks who balanced ledgers by hand prized that form, because small numbers are quick to add and easy to compare. When the Flemish engineer Simon Stevin pushed decimal notation in his 1585 booklet De Thiende, part of his pitch was that merchants and surveyors were worn out from hunting for shared factors and common denominators by hand. The hunt never really vanished, though. Every time you reduce a fraction or rebuild two of them onto a shared bottom, you are doing the same factor bookkeeping that countinghouses did for centuries, just faster, because Chapter 3 already handed you the \(\gcd\).

A locker company stamps a fraction onto every lock, and three of them are supposed to open the same door. The master number for that door is \(\tfrac{8}{14}\), and a clerk has to check the three locks fast, before the next batch rolls in. She is not allowed to reduce anything. Her only tool is the cross product test. For a stamped fraction \(\tfrac{c}{d}\) to truly equal \(\tfrac{8}{14}\), the two products \(8 \times d\) and \(14 \times c\) must come out the same. The three locks are stamped \(\tfrac{12}{21}\), then \(\tfrac{20}{35}\), then \(\tfrac{16}{30}\). She runs each one through the test and keeps a lock only when its two products match. How many of the three stamped fractions truly equal \(\tfrac{8}{14}\)?
hints: You do not need to simplify a single fraction here. For each stamped lock \(\tfrac{c}{d}\), build the two products \(8 \times d\) and \(14 \times c\) and just ask whether they land on the same number. Equal products mean the lock belongs, different products mean it does not.

Take them one at a time. For \(\tfrac{12}{21}\), compare \(8 \times 21\) with \(14 \times 12\). For \(\tfrac{20}{35}\), compare \(8 \times 35\) with \(14 \times 20\). For \(\tfrac{16}{30}\), compare \(8 \times 30\) with \(14 \times 16\). Count up how many of the three pairs come out matching.
blocks.sol:
The cross product test says \(\tfrac{c}{d}\) equals \(\tfrac{8}{14}\) exactly when \(8 \times d = 14 \times c\). So for each lock she builds two products and checks whether they match. No reducing required, which is the whole point of the trick.

First lock, \(\tfrac{12}{21}\). She computes \(8 \times 21 = 168\) and \(14 \times 12 = 168\). The two products match, so this lock truly equals \(\tfrac{8}{14}\). It stays.

Second lock, \(\tfrac{20}{35}\). She computes \(8 \times 35 = 280\) and \(14 \times 20 = 280\). Match again, so this lock belongs too.

Third lock, \(\tfrac{16}{30}\). She computes \(8 \times 30 = 240\) and \(14 \times 16 = 224\). These are close, only \(16\) apart, but close does not count. The products differ, so \(\tfrac{16}{30}\) is a near miss and does not equal \(\tfrac{8}{14}\). That lock gets tossed.

Two of the three pairs matched, so two of the stamped fractions are genuinely equal to the master number. The cross product test caught the impostor without her ever simplifying a thing.

\(\boxed{2}\)

blocks.x: A clockmaker is engraving a dial. Around its edge he carves the fractions \(\tfrac{1}{36}, \tfrac{2}{36}, \tfrac{3}{36}\), and so on, one tick for every whole number \(n\) from 1 to 36 over a fixed bottom of 36, ending at \(\tfrac{36}{36}\). He decides to gold plate only the ticks whose fraction is already in lowest terms, the ones that cannot be reduced at all. A fraction \(\tfrac{n}{36}\) is in lowest terms exactly when \(n\) and 36 share no common factor bigger than 1, which is the same as saying \(\gcd(n,36)=1\). Back in Chapter 3 you found that \(36 = 2\times 2\times 3\times 3\), so to survive, a top number \(n\) has to dodge being a multiple of 2 and dodge being a multiple of 3. How many of the ticks from \(\tfrac{1}{36}\) to \(\tfrac{36}{36}\) get gold plated?
hints: You are really counting how many numbers from 1 to 36 are coprime to 36, meaning they have no factor of 2 and no factor of 3. It is faster to count the ones that get thrown away than to check all 36 by hand. How many multiples of 2 are in 1 to 36, and how many multiples of 3?

There are 18 multiples of 2 and 12 multiples of 3 in the range, but the multiples of 6 got counted in both groups, so subtract them back once. That removes \(18 + 12 - 6 = 24\) numbers. Whatever is left out of 36 is your answer.
blocks.sol: A tick gets gold plated when its top number \(n\) shares no factor with 36 except 1. Since \(36 = 2\times 2\times 3\times 3\), the only prime factors lurking in 36 are 2 and 3. So \(n\) is safe exactly when it is not a multiple of 2 and not a multiple of 3. Instead of testing all 36 numbers, count the ones that fail and subtract. Among 1 to 36 there are \(\tfrac{36}{2}=18\) multiples of 2 and \(\tfrac{36}{3}=12\) multiples of 3. Adding those gives 30, but numbers like 6, 12, 18 got counted twice, once as a multiple of 2 and once as a multiple of 3. Those double counted numbers are exactly the multiples of 6, and there are \(\tfrac{36}{6}=6\) of them. So the count of numbers that have at least one bad factor is \(18 + 12 - 6 = 24\). The survivors are \(36 - 24 = 12\). You can sanity check by listing them, since the safe top numbers are \(1, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 25, 29, 31, 35\), and that is indeed twelve of them. The clockmaker gold plates \(\boxed{12}\) ticks.

review.x: A florist sets up a long window box and splits it into 3 equal sections. She fills only the first section with blossoms, so the flowers cover \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of the whole box. Later she wants to plan smaller clusters, so she lightly scores each section into 4 equal cells without moving a single flower. The box now holds \(3 \times 4 = 12\) tiny cells. If we rename \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) as a fraction with a bottom number of \(12\), how many of the 12 cells do the flowers cover?
hints: The total number of cells went from 3 to 12, which is 4 times as many. Whatever you do to the bottom of a fraction, do the same to the top so the value stays equal.
Multiply both the top and the bottom of \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) by \(4\). The top tells you how many of the 12 cells are filled.
review.sol: Scoring each of the 3 sections into 4 cells multiplies the total count by \(4\), turning 3 parts into \(3 \times 4 = 12\) parts. The flowers never moved, so the same patch of box is still covered. To keep the fraction equal while the bottom grows by a factor of \(4\), the top has to grow by the same factor of \(4\). $$\frac{1}{3} = \frac{1 \times 4}{3 \times 4} = \frac{4}{12}$$ So the filled section is now described as \(4\) of the \(12\) cells. You can check it the other way too, since \(\tfrac{4}{12}\) reduces back to \(\tfrac{1}{3}\). The flowers cover \(\boxed{4}\) cells.

review.x: A community choir is learning a new song, and the conductor wants exactly \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of the singers to hum the harmony line. Five small sections each report the fraction of their members who can hum harmony: \(\tfrac{8}{12}\), \(\tfrac{6}{10}\), \(\tfrac{10}{15}\), \(\tfrac{14}{21}\), and \(\tfrac{9}{12}\). How many of these five fractions name the same amount as \(\tfrac{2}{3}\)?
hints: Check each fraction one at a time against \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). You can cross multiply, so \(\tfrac{a}{b}=\tfrac{2}{3}\) exactly when \(3a = 2b\), or you can simplify each fraction to lowest terms and see if it becomes \(\tfrac{2}{3}\).

Test \(\tfrac{8}{12}\) with the cross multiply rule \(3 \times 8 = 24\) and \(2 \times 12 = 24\), so those two products match and that fraction counts. Do the same product check for the other four, then count up how many pass.
review.sol:
Two fractions name the same point exactly when their cross products are equal, so \(\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{2}{3}\) precisely when \(3a = 2b\).

Check each section.

\(\tfrac{8}{12}\): \(3 \times 8 = 24\) and \(2 \times 12 = 24\). Equal, so this matches.

\(\tfrac{6}{10}\): \(3 \times 6 = 18\) and \(2 \times 10 = 20\). Not equal, so this does not match.

\(\tfrac{10}{15}\): \(3 \times 10 = 30\) and \(2 \times 15 = 30\). Equal, so this matches.

\(\tfrac{14}{21}\): \(3 \times 14 = 42\) and \(2 \times 21 = 42\). Equal, so this matches.

\(\tfrac{9}{12}\): \(3 \times 9 = 27\) and \(2 \times 12 = 24\). Not equal, so this does not match.

Three of the five sections report a fraction equal to \(\tfrac{2}{3}\).

\(\boxed{3}\)

review.x: A long garden row is divided into 7 equal beds, and a soaker hose lies across exactly 2 of them, so it waters \(\tfrac{2}{7}\) of the row. Before planting, the crew re-cuts every bed into 4 equal patches, but they leave the hose resting in exactly the same spot. They want to describe the watered strip using these new, finer patches instead of the old beds. After the re-cut, how many equal patches make up the whole row?
hints: The hose has not moved, so the new fraction must still equal \(\tfrac{2}{7}\). Renaming a fraction with finer pieces means multiplying the top and the bottom by the same number.
Each of the 7 beds becomes 4 patches. How many patches does the whole row hold? That count is the new bottom number.
review.sol: Cutting every bed into smaller pieces does not move the hose, so the watered share is still \(\tfrac{2}{7}\). We are just renaming that same amount with finer patches. Each of the 7 beds splits into 4 patches, so the whole row now holds \(7 \times 4 = 28\) equal patches. That \(28\) is the new bottom number. To be sure the hose still covers the same strip, count its patches too. The 2 watered beds each split into 4, giving \(2 \times 4 = 8\) patches, so the watered share is \(\tfrac{8}{28}\). And \(\tfrac{8}{28}\) reduces right back to \(\tfrac{2}{7}\), which confirms nothing about the coverage changed, only the names of the pieces. The whole row is made of \(\boxed{28}\) equal patches.

review.x: A warehouse robot logs each jar's fill level as a fraction of its full mark. One jar comes back tagged as \(\tfrac{10}{25}\) full. The robot prefers the shortest possible label, so it wants the same amount written with the smallest whole numbers on top and bottom. Reduce \(\tfrac{10}{25}\) to lowest terms by dividing the top and bottom by their common divisor. Write the simplest name as a fraction \(a/b\).
hints: What is the largest number that divides evenly into both 10 and 25? Both end in either 0 or 5, so 5 goes into each.
Divide the top and the bottom by 5. Ten split into groups of five gives the new top, and twenty-five split into groups of five gives the new bottom.
review.sol: A fraction stays the same value when you divide the top and bottom by the same number, so I look for a factor they share. Both \(10\) and \(25\) are multiples of \(5\), and \(5\) is the largest number that divides both, so \(\gcd(10,25)=5\). Dividing each part by \(5\) gives \(10\div 5 = 2\) on top and \(25\div 5 = 5\) on the bottom. That leaves \(\tfrac{2}{5}\), and since \(2\) and \(5\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), it cannot shrink any further. The robot's shortest label is \(\boxed{2/5}\).

review.x: A class survey asks whether the school should start ten minutes later. The results come back with \(30\) yes votes out of \(42\) total responses, so the fraction of yes votes is \(\tfrac{30}{42}\). Use what you learned in Chapter 3 to find \(\gcd(30,42)\), then divide both the top and bottom by that number in a single step to write \(\tfrac{30}{42}\) in lowest terms. What fraction \(a/b\) do you get?
hints: First find the greatest common divisor. List what divides both \(30\) and \(42\), or use the factor pairs from Chapter 3. The largest number that goes into both is \(6\).
Now divide the top and bottom by that \(6\) at the same time. Compute \(30 \div 6\) for the new numerator and \(42 \div 6\) for the new denominator.
review.sol: The fastest way to lowest terms is to peel off the whole common factor at once instead of chipping away at it. So start by finding \(\gcd(30,42)\). Both numbers are even, and looking closer, \(6\) divides \(30\) since \(30 = 6 \times 5\), and \(6\) divides \(42\) since \(42 = 6 \times 7\). Nothing larger divides both, so \(\gcd(30,42) = 6\). Now divide the top and bottom by that \(6\) in one step. $$\frac{30}{42} = \frac{30 \div 6}{42 \div 6} = \frac{5}{7}$$ Since \(5\) and \(7\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), the fraction is fully reduced. \(\boxed{5/7}\)

review.x: A bottle in a chemistry kit has a faded label that reads \(\tfrac{15}{28}\). Before you bother trying to reduce it, you want to know whether it is already in lowest terms. A fraction is already as simple as it gets exactly when the only whole number that divides both the top and the bottom is \(1\), in other words when \(\gcd\) of the two numbers is \(1\). Find \(\gcd(15, 28)\).
hints: List the divisors of \(15\). They are \(1\), \(3\), \(5\), and \(15\). Now check which of those also divide \(28\) evenly.

\(28\) breaks into \(2 \times 2 \times 7\), so its only divisors are \(1\), \(2\), \(4\), \(7\), \(14\), and \(28\). Compare that list with the divisors of \(15\) and find the largest number that shows up in both.
review.sol: Break each number into its prime factors. The top, \(15\), is \(3 \times 5\). The bottom, \(28\), is \(2 \times 2 \times 7\). Now look at the two lists of primes side by side. One uses \(3\) and \(5\), the other uses \(2\) and \(7\). They share no prime at all. When two numbers have no prime factor in common, the only positive whole number dividing both of them is \(1\), so \(\gcd(15, 28) = 1\). That tells you the label \(\tfrac{15}{28}\) is already in lowest terms, and there is nothing left to cancel. The gcd you were asked for is \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: A data sheet keeps every reading written over a fixed bottom of \(48\), so the values line up neatly when you compare them. A new reading arrives written as \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), and before it can be filed the sheet has to rewrite it with a bottom of exactly \(48\). To do that you find the number \(k\) where \(12 \times k = 48\), then scale the top by that same \(k\) so the value stays the same. What top number gets stored over \(48\)?
hints: First figure out what you multiply the bottom by. Since \(12 \times k = 48\), you need \(k = 48 \div 12\).
Whatever you multiply the bottom by, you must multiply the top by the same amount, or the value changes. Multiply \(7\) by that same \(k\).
review.sol: The bottom has to grow from \(12\) to \(48\), so first find the scaling number \(k\). Since \(12 \times k = 48\), we get \(k = 48 \div 12 = 4\). The trick with equivalent fractions is that the value only stays the same if you scale the top and the bottom by the exact same number, so you multiply the top by \(4\) too. That gives a top of \(7 \times 4 = 28\), which means \(\tfrac{7}{12} = \tfrac{28}{48}\). You can check it by reducing \(\tfrac{28}{48}\), dividing both parts by \(4\) lands you right back at \(\tfrac{7}{12}\). The number stored over \(48\) is \(\boxed{28}\).

review.x: A drone's status light shows its battery at \(\tfrac{56}{64}\) charged. That number is correct, but it is bulky. To store it neatly, the software reduces it to lowest terms by dividing the top and bottom by their greatest common divisor. What is \(\tfrac{56}{64}\) written in lowest terms, as a fraction \(a/b\)?
hints: Find the greatest common divisor of 56 and 64. Both are even, and you can keep pulling out factors of 2.
The gcd of 56 and 64 is \(8\). Divide the top by \(8\) and the bottom by \(8\).
review.sol: A fraction is in lowest terms when the top and bottom share no common divisor larger than \(1\). So the job is to find the greatest common divisor of \(56\) and \(64\), then divide both by it. Both numbers are even, and in fact both are built from a lot of \(2\)s. We have \(56 = 8 \times 7\) and \(64 = 8 \times 8\), so their greatest common divisor is \(8\). Dividing both parts by \(8\) gives $$\frac{56}{64} = \frac{56 \div 8}{64 \div 8} = \frac{7}{8}.$$ Now \(7\) is prime and \(8\) is not a multiple of \(7\), so they share no factor beyond \(1\). The battery is \(\boxed{7/8}\) charged.

review.x: Two sensors watch the same long laser beam and each reports how much of it is lit. The first sensor reads the lit portion as \(\tfrac{8}{14}\) of the beam. The second sensor reads it as \(\tfrac{12}{21}\) of the beam. Before deciding whether the sensors agree, you run the cross-multiply test. Compare \(8 \times 21\) on one side against \(14 \times 12\) on the other, and only count the two readings as the same point on the beam if those two products come out equal. Do the two sensors name the same point? Enter yes or no.
hints: The cross-multiply test ignores the reduced forms and just multiplies across the diagonals. Work out \(8 \times 21\) for one diagonal and \(14 \times 12\) for the other.
Both products land on \(168\). When the two cross products match exactly, the fractions are equivalent, so the readings point to the very same spot.
review.sol: The cross-multiply test takes the top of one fraction times the bottom of the other and checks both diagonals against each other. Run the first diagonal, \(8 \times 21 = 168\). Now run the second diagonal, \(14 \times 12 = 168\). The two products are the same, \(168 = 168\), so the test says the readings are equivalent. You can feel why by reducing each one. Dividing \(\tfrac{8}{14}\) by \(2\) gives \(\tfrac{4}{7}\), and dividing \(\tfrac{12}{21}\) by \(3\) also gives \(\tfrac{4}{7}\). Both sensors are really reporting the same fraction in disguise, so they do name the same point on the beam. \(\boxed{yes}\)

review.x: A weather sensor logs each reading as a single point on a 0 to 40 dial, stored internally as the fraction \(\dfrac{x}{40}\). One morning the needle sits at exactly \(\dfrac{5}{8}\) of the way around, so the stored fraction and \(\dfrac{5}{8}\) must name the very same point. Because two equivalent fractions stay equal when you cross-multiply, that point gives \(5 \times 40 = 8 \times x\). What is the top number \(x\)?
hints: Cross-multiplying \(\dfrac{x}{40} = \dfrac{5}{8}\) means the diagonal products match, so \(8 \times x = 5 \times 40\). Work out the right side first.
You now have \(8 \times x = 200\). To free \(x\), undo the multiplication by dividing both sides by \(8\).
review.sol: Equivalent fractions point to the same spot, so \(\dfrac{x}{40}\) and \(\dfrac{5}{8}\) have to be equal. Cross-multiplying lines up the two diagonals, giving \(8 \times x = 5 \times 40\). The right side is \(5 \times 40 = 200\), so \(8 \times x = 200\). Dividing both sides by \(8\) leaves \(x = \dfrac{200}{8} = 25\). A quick check confirms it, since \(\dfrac{25}{40}\) reduces by \(5\) to \(\dfrac{5}{8}\), the value we started with. So the stored top number is \(\boxed{25}\).

review.x: A municipal recycling center logs every glass jar that comes down the sorting belt during one shift. By the end of the shift the scanner has counted \(252\) jars in total, and \(162\) of them came back clean enough to send straight to the furnace. The supervisor wants this share written as a fraction in lowest terms for the daily report. Reduce \(\tfrac{162}{252}\) all the way to its simplest form \(\tfrac{a}{b}\). What is the reduced fraction?
hints: Break both \(162\) and \(252\) all the way down into primes. Once you see which primes appear in both lists, you can pair them up to find the greatest common divisor (gcd).
The shared primes are one \(2\) and two \(3\)s, so the gcd is \(2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18\). Divide the top and bottom by \(18\).
review.sol:
Start by breaking each number all the way into primes.

$$162 = 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 2 \times 3^4$$

$$252 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 7 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 7$$

Now line up the primes the two numbers share. Both have a single \(2\) to spare, and both carry at least two \(3\)s. So the shared primes are one \(2\) and two \(3\)s. Multiply those together to get the greatest common divisor (gcd).

$$\gcd(162, 252) = 2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18$$

Divide the numerator and denominator by \(18\).

$$\frac{162}{252} = \frac{162 \div 18}{252 \div 18} = \frac{9}{14}$$

Since \(9 = 3^2\) and \(14 = 2 \times 7\) share no primes, the fraction is fully reduced. The clean-jar share is \(\boxed{9/14}\).

review.x: A model railway club builds wheel plates, and every plate is stamped with a fraction printed as a top number over a bottom number. The machine can only carve a plate whose bottom number is a whole number no larger than \(80\). The club wants one plate for every fraction equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\), and \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) is already in lowest terms, so the only bottoms that give a fraction equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) are the multiples of \(8\). How many fractions equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) have a bottom number that is a whole number no larger than \(80\)?
hints: Every fraction equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) comes from multiplying both \(3\) and \(8\) by the same whole number, so the bottom is always a multiple of \(8\). Which multiples of \(8\) are no bigger than \(80\)?
List them and count: \(8, 16, 24, \dots\) up to \(80\). Or just notice that the multiples of \(8\) up to \(80\) are \(8\times 1\) through \(8\times ?\).
review.sol: A fraction equal to \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) is built by multiplying the top and the bottom by the same whole number \(k\), giving \(\tfrac{3k}{8k}\). Since \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) is already in lowest terms, the bottom of any equal fraction is exactly a multiple of \(8\), namely \(8k\). We need \(8k\) to be a whole number no larger than \(80\), so \(8k \le 80\), which means \(k \le 10\). The allowed values are \(k = 1, 2, 3, \dots, 10\), giving the bottoms \(8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64, 72, 80\). That is \(10\) values of \(k\), so there are \(\boxed{10}\) such fractions.

review.x: A craft fair has a spinning prize wheel. Around its rim sit 30 little slots, and slot number \(n\) is painted with the fraction \(\frac{n}{30}\), so the slots read \(\frac{1}{30}, \frac{2}{30}, \frac{3}{30}\), all the way up to \(\frac{30}{30}\). The judge wants to award a sticker to every slot whose fraction is already in lowest terms, meaning the top and bottom share no common factor bigger than 1. A fraction \(\frac{n}{30}\) is in lowest terms exactly when \(\gcd(n, 30) = 1\). Since \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\), a winning \(n\) must dodge every multiple of 2, every multiple of 3, and every multiple of 5. How many of the 30 slots get a sticker?
hints: You only need to keep the values of \(n\) that share no factor with 30. Since \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\), cross off every even number, every multiple of 3, and every multiple of 5 from the list 1 through 30.

Start with all 30 numbers, then remove the 15 evens, then remove the multiples of 3 that are still left, then the multiples of 5 that are still left. Count what survives. You can also just list the survivors directly and they will all be numbers ending in 1, 3, 7, or 9 that are not multiples of 3.
review.sol: A slot wins when \(\gcd(n, 30) = 1\), and because \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\), the winning \(n\) cannot be a multiple of 2, of 3, or of 5. The cleanest way is to just hunt for the survivors. Any even number is out, so I only look at odd numbers. Any multiple of 5 is out, which removes 5, 15, 25. Any multiple of 3 is out, which removes 3, 9, 15, 21, 27. What is left from 1 to 30 is \(1, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 29\). Counting those gives 8 winners. There is also a slick shortcut once you notice the factors. Of the 30 numbers, exactly half are even, so 15 survive the "no 2" rule. Of those that survive, one third would be multiples of 3 and one fifth would be multiples of 5, and the pattern of crossing-out leaves a fraction \(\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{5}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{5} = \tfrac{8}{30}\) of the whole, and \(\tfrac{8}{30}\) of 30 is \(8\). Both roads land in the same place. $$\boxed{8}$$

blocks.x:
A long strip of fresh bread dough rests on a floured table. The baker calls the whole strip \(1\), one undivided thing, and hands one third of it to her apprentice to shape into rolls. So far the picture is simple. The strip got split into three equal lengths, and the apprentice walked off with one of them, a clean \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of the whole.

Then he changes his mind. He does not use all of what he was handed. He sets aside half of his piece to practice a braid, and shapes the leftover into rolls. Watch the two cuts in order. The first cut split the strip into three. The second cut split his one piece in two. The braid is made from half of a third of the original strip.

Hold two things in your head at the same time. First, the braid is a real, fixed amount of dough. You could lift it onto a scale and read off a number. It is not vague, it is a definite slice of the whole strip. Second, it is plainly smaller than the third he began with, because he reached into that third and kept only a part of it. Taking a part of a part lands you somewhere smaller, every time.

Here is the honest question, and the lesson is not going to answer it yet. That braid is itself some single fraction of the whole strip, one clean address like one over something. Which fraction is it? And is there a rule that tells you the answer without standing over the dough and re-cutting it by hand every time? Notice the small word doing the linking. Half of a third. That little word of is quietly asking you to do some arithmetic. By the end of the lesson that word turns into something you already know how to do, and the very same machinery will run backwards to handle dividing too. Two pictures will settle the whole thing, one for taking parts and one for fitting things inside.

A community garden bed is laid out as 12 equal squares of soil, and every square is planted. A late frost is moving in tonight, so the gardener decides to cover two thirds of the bed with a sheet of cloth to keep those plants warm. Here is the useful trick. When we say two thirds of 12, that little word of is telling us to do something. Picture the 12 squares split into three equal groups, then keep two of those groups under the cloth. How many of the 12 squares end up covered?
hints: The bottom number of \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) tells you how many equal groups to split the 12 squares into. Make the groups first, before you worry about how many to keep.
blocks.sol: The word of here is a quiet instruction. Two thirds of 12 means we want \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of the 12 squares, and the way to find a fraction of an amount is to split, then keep. The bottom number, the 3, says split the 12 squares into three equal groups. \(12 \div 3 = 4\), so each group is 4 squares. The top number, the 2, says keep two of those groups. Two groups of 4 squares is \(2 \times 4 = 8\) squares under the cloth. Notice what just happened. Finding \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of 12 came out exactly the same as \(\tfrac{2}{3} \times 12 = \tfrac{2 \times 12}{3} = \tfrac{24}{3} = 8\). The little word of behaved precisely like a multiply sign, and it will keep doing that even when the amount you take a part of is itself a fraction. $$\boxed{8}$$

blocks.x: Back to a strip, the same shape the dough hook stretched out earlier. A long roll of garden netting is the whole, its full length counts as 1. A worker folds the roll into 4 equal panels and unrolls just one of them to fix a hole, so that single panel is \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) of all the netting. The hole is small though, so inside that one quarter she marks off 3 equal sub panels and patches with just one of them. Walk the two cuts. The first cut splits the whole roll into 4 equal panels. The second cut splits the one panel she is working in into 3 equal slivers, and she keeps one sliver. Now picture carrying that second set of cuts all the way across the roll, so every one of the 4 panels gets sliced into 3 slivers the same size as hers. If the whole roll is now covered in equal little slivers, her patch is exactly one of them. The patch she used is one over what number, written as a fraction of the whole netting? Write it as a/b.
hints: You do not need to multiply anything yet, just count slivers. Each of the 4 equal panels gets cut into 3 equal slivers. Lay the slivers end to end down the whole roll and count how many there are in total.
There are \(4 \times 3 = 12\) equal slivers covering the whole roll, and they are all the same size. Her patch is one of those 12 equal pieces, so it is one part out of 12 of the whole.
blocks.sol: Start with the panel she actually unrolled. It is one of 4 equal panels, so it is \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) of the whole roll. She does not use the whole panel though. She cuts that panel into 3 equal slivers and keeps 1, so she uses \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of that quarter. The question is really asking for \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) of \(\tfrac{1}{4}\) of the roll. Here is the picture that nails it. When you carry the 3-way cut across all 4 panels, the second cut tripled the number of pieces. You went from 4 equal panels to \(4 \times 3 = 12\) equal slivers, and every sliver is identical. Her patch is exactly one of those 12 equal slivers, so it is \(\tfrac{1}{12}\) of the whole roll. That matches the rule for a unit fraction of a unit fraction. Taking \(\tfrac{1}{a}\) of \(\tfrac{1}{b}\) means cutting the whole into \(a\) parts and then each part into \(b\) parts, which leaves \(a \times b\) equal pieces, so the result is \(\tfrac{1}{ab}\). With \(a = 4\) and \(b = 3\) that gives \(\tfrac{1}{4 \times 3} = \tfrac{1}{12}\). $$\boxed{1/12}$$

blocks.x: When you take a part of a part, you are cutting and then cutting again inside just one of the pieces. The first cut splits the whole into \(a\) pieces, and cutting each of those into \(b\) pieces chops the whole into \(a \times b\) tiny equal pieces, and you keep exactly one. That is why \(\frac{1}{a}\) of \(\frac{1}{b}\) lands on \(\frac{1}{ab}\), and it is also the secret the word of has been holding all along. Of means multiply, the same as it did when you took two thirds of \(12\), and a fraction of a fraction always comes out smaller, which is exactly why the dough braid and the netting patch shrank to such little slivers.

A square plot of land is one whole field. A surveyor walks in with her ruler and starts slicing. First she cuts the field into 5 equal horizontal bands running left to right, one band for each crop in her rotation, and she hands the very top band to clover. So far so good, the clover band is \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of the field. Now she steps inside that single clover band and cuts it into 6 equal vertical sections, and today she only has time to water one of them. Here is the trick. Imagine those vertical cuts didn't stop at the clover band. Imagine all 6 of them sliced straight down through every band, and all 5 horizontal cuts ran clear across, so the whole field becomes a tidy grid of identical little cells. The watered patch is exactly one of those cells. What fraction of the whole field is the watered patch? Write your answer as a/b.
hints: The 5 horizontal cuts and the 6 vertical cuts together carve the field into a grid. Picture the grid and count how many cells fit across the field and how many fit down it. Every cell is the same size, and the watered patch is just one of them.
A grid that is 5 rows tall and 6 columns wide holds \(5 \times 6\) cells in total, and each one is an equal share of the whole field. The watered patch is a single cell, so it is 1 out of that total.
blocks.sol:
The watered patch is one section of one band, so it is a cut sitting inside another cut. Start with the clover band. It is one of 5 equal bands, so the band is \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of the field. The watered piece is one of 6 equal sections of that band, so it is \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) of the band. That makes the patch \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) of \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of the whole field, which is \(\tfrac{1}{6}\times\tfrac{1}{5}=\tfrac{1}{30}\).

The grid picture says the same thing in one glance. The 5 horizontal cuts make 5 rows, the 6 vertical cuts make 6 columns, and where they cross the field becomes a grid of \(5\times 6 = 30\) identical cells. The watered patch is exactly one of those 30 equal cells, so it is 1 out of 30 of the whole field. $$\boxed{1/30}$$

blocks.cap: Start with one whole square. Slicing the width into 3 equal columns and keeping the left 2 marks off \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). Slicing the height into 5 equal rows and keeping the bottom 3 marks off \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Together the cuts break the square into \(3\times 5 = 15\) little cells. Taking \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of the \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) strip means keeping only the part of that strip that also sits in the left \(\tfrac{2}{3}\), and that overlap is the gold block, \(2\times 3 = 6\) cells. So \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times\tfrac{3}{5}=\tfrac{6}{15}\). The tops multiplied to count the gold cells, and the bottoms multiplied to count all the cells.

blocks.x: Step back into the very square you just drew. A mosaic artist treats the whole panel as the number \(1\) and rules it into a grid, \(3\) equal columns across and \(5\) equal rows up. First she sets pale glass in the left \(2\) of the \(3\) columns, which covers two thirds of the width. Then she lays gold leaf in the bottom \(3\) of the \(5\) rows, which covers three fifths of the height. The little cells where the pale glass and the gold leaf both land are exactly the part that is two thirds of the way across and three fifths of the way up. That overlap region is two thirds of three fifths. Count the cells. Multiply the tops together for the overlap count and the bottoms together for the total, then bring it down to lowest terms using the gcd, the same move you practiced in Chapter 3. What fraction of the whole panel does the overlap cover, fully reduced? Write it as a/b.
hints: The grid is \(3\) columns wide and \(5\) rows tall, so the whole panel is cut into \(3 \times 5\) equal cells. The overlap is the block that is both in the pale \(2\) columns and in the gold \(3\) rows. How many columns wide is that block, and how many rows tall? Those two counts are the tops of your fractions.

Multiply tops with tops and bottoms with bottoms. Two thirds of three fifths is \(\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{5} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3 \times 5} = \frac{6}{15}\). Now find \(\gcd(6,15)\) and divide both numbers by it.
blocks.sol: Read the picture as a count. The panel is sliced into \(3\) columns and \(5\) rows, so there are \(3 \times 5 = 15\) equal cells, and each cell is \(\tfrac{1}{15}\) of the whole. The pale glass fills the left \(2\) columns, and the gold leaf fills the bottom \(3\) rows. The overlap is the rectangle that is \(2\) columns wide and \(3\) rows tall, so it holds \(2 \times 3 = 6\) cells. That is why multiplying fractions multiplies the tops and multiplies the bottoms, the tops count how wide and tall the overlap block is, and the bottoms count how the whole was cut up. So \(\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{5} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3 \times 5} = \frac{6}{15}\). Now reduce. The gcd of \(6\) and \(15\) is \(3\), and dividing both by \(3\) gives \(\frac{6 \div 3}{15 \div 3} = \frac{2}{5}\). The overlap covers \(\frac{2}{5}\) of the panel, which makes sense, two thirds of three fifths should land a little under half. $$\boxed{2/5}$$

blocks.x: So here is the rule we just read straight off the grid. To multiply two fractions, you multiply the top numbers to get the new top, and you multiply the bottom numbers to get the new bottom. The new top counts the overlap, so many cells across times so many cells up, and the new bottom counts the whole square, so many columns times so many rows, which is the very same story as the netting strip where one cut of size \(a\) and another of size \(b\) left \(a\times b\) little pieces. And notice this one rule quietly swallows everything from before, since a whole number is just that number over \(1\), and taking a fraction of an amount is exactly this same multiply.

A scout troop is making trail mix to sell at a fundraiser. One single batch calls for \(\frac{5}{8}\) of a cup of dried cherries. The troop plans to make 6 identical batches, and they want to know how many cups of dried cherries they will use all together. Notice that nothing here is being cut into a part of a part. The same \(\frac{5}{8}\) of a cup gets measured out again and again, once for each batch. How many cups of dried cherries do they need in total? Leave your answer as an improper fraction.
hints: Six batches means you lay down \(\frac{5}{8}\) of a cup, then another \(\frac{5}{8}\), and so on, 6 times over. The eighths are all the same size, so think about how many eighths you end up with once you have collected 6 of these scoops.

Write the 6 as \(\frac{6}{1}\) and multiply straight across, tops times tops and bottoms times bottoms. The bottom stays 8 because each scoop is still an eighth of a cup, only the count of eighths changed. So you get \(\frac{6 \times 5}{1 \times 8}\), then simplify.
blocks.sol:
Six batches is just \(\frac{5}{8}\) of a cup repeated 6 times. The size of each scoop never changes, so every piece is still one eighth of a cup. What grows is how many eighths you have collected.

Each batch brings 5 eighths. After 6 batches you have \(6 \times 5 = 30\) eighths of a cup. The bottom of the fraction stays 8 the whole time, because an eighth is still an eighth no matter how many you pile up.

You can see this as the whole number 6 written as \(\frac{6}{1}\), then multiplied across.
$$\frac{6}{1} \times \frac{5}{8} = \frac{6 \times 5}{1 \times 8} = \frac{30}{8}$$

Now simplify. Both 30 and 8 share a factor of 2, so \(\frac{30}{8} = \frac{15}{4}\).

The troop needs $$\boxed{15/4}$$ cups of dried cherries in all.

blocks.x: A paint mixer is preparing a batch. The recipe calls for \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) of a tank of base, and the tank in front of it is only \(\tfrac{4}{9}\) full of base right now. So the amount of base the mixer can actually use is \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) of that \(\tfrac{4}{9}\), which means \(\tfrac{3}{8} \times \tfrac{4}{9}\). You could multiply straight across and get \(\tfrac{12}{72}\), but that drops you into big numbers you then have to reduce. There is a cleaner path. Look across the two fractions before you multiply. Notice the \(4\) sitting on top of the second fraction and the \(8\) sitting on the bottom of the first, and notice the \(3\) on top of the first and the \(9\) on the bottom of the second. Each pair shares a factor you can divide out first. What is \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) of \(\tfrac{4}{9}\), fully reduced, written as a/b?
hints: When you multiply fractions, everything on top forms one product and everything on the bottom forms another, so a number on top and a number on the bottom can cancel a shared factor even if they started in different fractions. Look at the \(4\) and the \(8\), and separately at the \(3\) and the \(9\). What does each pair share?

The \(4\) and \(8\) share a factor of \(4\): replace them with \(1\) and \(2\). The \(3\) and \(9\) share a factor of \(3\): replace them with \(1\) and \(3\). Now the leftovers are \(\tfrac{1}{2} \times \tfrac{1}{3}\). Multiply those small numbers.
blocks.sol:
Multiplying fractions joins all the tops into one product and all the bottoms into another, so \(\tfrac{3}{8} \times \tfrac{4}{9}\) is really \(\dfrac{3 \times 4}{8 \times 9}\). That means any top factor and any bottom factor are sitting in the same big fraction together, and a factor they share can be divided out no matter which fraction it came from.

Hunt for those shared factors across the two fractions. The \(4\) on a top and the \(8\) on a bottom share a \(4\), so dividing both by \(4\) turns them into \(1\) and \(2\). The \(3\) on a top and the \(9\) on a bottom share a \(3\), so dividing both by \(3\) turns them into \(1\) and \(3\). Both cancellations cross between the two fractions, which is exactly what you are allowed to do.

What is left is tiny. The tops are \(1\) and \(1\), and the bottoms are \(2\) and \(3\), so the product is \(\dfrac{1 \times 1}{2 \times 3} = \dfrac{1}{6}\). No reducing needed at the end, because you did the reducing up front.

If you had multiplied straight across you would have reached \(\tfrac{12}{72}\), which is the same value, just dressed in larger clothes. Cancelling first kept the numbers small the whole way through.

$$\boxed{1/6}$$

blocks.x: When I multiply fractions, every top eventually piles into one big top and every bottom piles into one big bottom, so it does not matter which fraction a number started in. That means if some number up top and some number down low share a factor, I can divide both by that factor right now, before I multiply anything, even when one of them is in the left fraction and the other is in the right. A matched factor like \(4\) over \(4\) would only ever cancel to \(1\), so pulling it out early just spares me from wrestling with huge numbers. It is the same self-cancelling I did when renaming fractions in 4.2, except this time the answer lands already reduced, with the \(\gcd\) from Chapter 3 quietly doing the work mid-multiplication.

A water reservoir sets each day's safe draw as a fraction of the day before. Today's safe draw is \(\tfrac{14}{15}\) of yesterday's allowance, and yesterday's allowance was \(\tfrac{25}{21}\) of the base ration. So today's draw, measured against the base ration, is \(\tfrac{14}{15}\times\tfrac{25}{21}\). You could multiply straight across and get \(\tfrac{350}{315}\), but that is a clumsy pile of numbers to reduce afterward. Instead, break every number into its prime factors and strike out any factor that shows up on a top and a bottom, even when the top and bottom belong to different fractions. What is the product, fully reduced, written as a/b?
hints: Before you multiply anything, look across the two fractions for shared factors. The 14 on top and the 21 on the bottom both hold a 7. The 25 on top and the 15 on the bottom both hold a 5. A factor on any top cancels with the same factor on any bottom, no matter which fraction it sits in.

Write it as \(\dfrac{2\cdot 7}{3\cdot 5}\times\dfrac{5\cdot 5}{3\cdot 7}\). Cross out the matching \(7\) (from 14 and 21) and one matching \(5\) (from 25 and 15). What survives on top is \(2\cdot 5\) and on the bottom is \(3\cdot 3\). Multiply each side.
blocks.sol: Multiplying tops and bottoms straight away gives \(\frac{14\times 25}{15\times 21}=\frac{350}{315}\), and now you are stuck reducing a big fraction. The trick is to cancel before you multiply, because in a product of fractions every top factor is paired against every bottom factor. \nBreak each number into primes. \(14=2\cdot 7\), \(25=5\cdot 5\), \(15=3\cdot 5\), and \(21=3\cdot 7\). So the product is $$\frac{2\cdot 7}{3\cdot 5}\times\frac{5\cdot 5}{3\cdot 7}=\frac{2\cdot 7\cdot 5\cdot 5}{3\cdot 5\cdot 3\cdot 7}.$$\nNow strike out a factor on top against the same factor on the bottom. The \(7\) (from \(14\)) cancels the \(7\) (from \(21\)), and one \(5\) (from \(25\)) cancels the \(5\) (from \(15\)). What remains on top is \(2\cdot 5=10\), and what remains on the bottom is \(3\cdot 3=9\). \nThat leaves \(\frac{10}{9}\), already in lowest terms since \(\gcd(10,9)=1\). The same answer you would get from \(\frac{350}{315}\) after a lot more work, with none of the mess. \(\boxed{10/9}\)

blocks.x: A spore drifts in on the wind and lands, and what happens next is a relay. The colony has to clear four growth stages, and each stage is brutal in its own quiet way. Only a fraction of whatever survived the stage before is passed along to the next. \nStage one lets through \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) of the spores. Stage two lets through \(\tfrac{4}{5}\) of those. Stage three lets through \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of those. Stage four lets through \(\tfrac{6}{7}\) of those. To get the fraction that makes it all the way through, you multiply the four survival rates together, $$\frac{3}{4}\times\frac{4}{5}\times\frac{5}{6}\times\frac{6}{7}.$$\nLook at the chain for a second before you reach for a pencil. The bottom of each fraction is the top of the next one, a little staircase climbing 4, 5, 6, 7 across the denominators while 3, 4, 5, 6 climb across the numerators. That overlap is not an accident, and it is about to do almost all the work for you. What single fully reduced fraction, written as a/b, does the whole chain equal?
hints: Do not multiply the tops into one giant number and the bottoms into another. Instead, write all four numerators on one line over all four denominators on one line, like \(\dfrac{3\times 4\times 5\times 6}{4\times 5\times 6\times 7}\), and then hunt for numbers that appear in both rows.

A factor on top cancels the same factor on the bottom. The \(4\) shows up on top and on bottom, so it vanishes. So does the \(5\). So does the \(6\). Cross all three pairs out and read off whatever is still standing on top and on bottom.
blocks.sol:
The slow way is to multiply everything out, but the chain is begging us not to. Stack all four numerators over all four denominators in one big fraction,
$$\frac{3}{4}\times\frac{4}{5}\times\frac{5}{6}\times\frac{6}{7}=\frac{3\times 4\times 5\times 6}{4\times 5\times 6\times 7}.$$\nNow the staircase pays off. The \(4\) sits on top and also on the bottom, so that pair cancels to \(1\). The \(5\) does the same. The \(6\) does the same. Three matching pairs quietly delete themselves,
$$\frac{3\times \cancel{4}\times \cancel{5}\times \cancel{6}}{\cancel{4}\times \cancel{5}\times \cancel{6}\times 7}=\frac{3}{7}.$$\nWhat survives is exactly the first numerator and the last denominator, the two ends of the chain that had no partner to cancel against. Everything in the middle was both a top and a bottom, so it washed out. And \(\tfrac{3}{7}\) is already in lowest terms, since \(3\) and \(7\) share no factor bigger than \(1\). The colony passes along \(\;\boxed{\dfrac{3}{7}}\;\) of the spores it started with.

blocks.x: That chain you just watched collapse has a name. It is called a telescoping product, after the old folding spyglass that slides shut into almost nothing when you push it closed. Each number on top found its identical twin one step down on the bottom, the pair cancelled to \(1\), and the long line of factors folded in on itself until only the two outermost numbers survived, the very first top and the very last bottom. It is one of the quietest, most satisfying sights in arithmetic, a row of ugly looking factors calmly destroying itself in pairs. Once you notice that each bottom matches the next top, you can fold the whole thing shut in your head without ever multiplying a single pair.

A warehouse receives \(200\) liters of concentrate. Quality control immediately holds back three quarters of the shipment as a testing reserve. Then, out of that reserve, two fifths is set aside for the long term archive and the rest goes back into rotation. Read the situation exactly as it is written, two fifths of three quarters of \(200\) liters, which is three multiplications strung into a single product. Try cancelling as you go rather than waiting until the very end, and the numbers stay friendly the whole way. How many liters go to the long term archive?
hints: Each of is a multiplication. So the archive amount is \(\frac{2}{5}\times\frac{3}{4}\times 200\). You do not have to compute the reserve as a separate number first, you can write all three factors in one product and simplify before multiplying.

Pair up a numerator with a denominator before you multiply anything out. The \(200\) and the \(5\) cancel to leave \(40\), and the \(4\) divides into that \(40\) to leave \(10\). Now you are just left with \(2\times 3\times 10\).
blocks.sol: Every of here means multiply, so the whole chain becomes one product. $$\frac{2}{5}\times\frac{3}{4}\times 200.$$ Instead of working it left to right, treat the \(200\) as \(\frac{200}{1}\) and look for pairs to cancel across the top and bottom. The \(5\) on the bottom goes into the \(200\) on top, turning \(200\) into \(40\). Then the \(4\) on the bottom goes into that \(40\), turning it into \(10\). What survives is gentle. $$2\times 3\times 10 = 60.$$ So three quarters of \(200\) was \(150\) liters of reserve, and two fifths of that reserve is the archive, and it lands on the same clean number. The warehouse sends \(\boxed{60}\) liters to the long term archive.

blocks.x: Every time you hear the word of, you can swap in a times sign, and a long string of "two thirds of three fifths of..." is just one big multiplication waiting to happen. To do it, you multiply across the tops and across the bottoms, and you make life easy by cancelling any factor a top shares with a bottom before you ever multiply. A whole number is welcome too, it just shows up as itself over \(1\). And here is the quiet shock that showed up again and again, when you multiply two proper fractions the answer is smaller than both of them, because taking a part of an already partial amount can only leave you with less.

Look back at where we started. Half of a third of the dough. Then part after part after that, and every single one moved the same direction. You held an amount, you reached in and took a piece of it, and the thing you were left with was smaller than what you began with. That is what of does. It walks you forward into a part of a part. But arithmetic never builds a road that only goes one way. Every operation has a return trip, and we are about to take it. If multiplying answers the question what is a part of this, then somewhere there is a backward question that multiplying quietly answers in reverse, and that backward question is division. Here is the cleanest way to feel it, before any rule shows up. Forget the rules for a second and just count. How many small things fit inside a big thing? A worker has a 3 cup jug and a quarter cup scoop, and wonders how many scoops it takes to empty the jug. A ribbon is 6 feet long and each bow eats up two thirds of a foot, so how many bows can you tie? Notice these are not strange new machines dropped out of nowhere. They are multiplication running backwards, and underneath they are all the same plain question, how many of THESE fit inside THAT. That question is division. And the tool that turns it back into a multiply, that flips the whole thing around and lets you just multiply again, is a number we have not met yet.

A stretching machine takes any length you feed it and scales it by \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), so a stick that goes in comes out a little shorter. You want a second machine that perfectly undoes the first one, returning every length to exactly where it started. If you run a stick through the first machine and then the second, the two scalings multiply together, and to leave a length unchanged that product has to equal \(1\). So you need the fraction that multiplies with \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) to give exactly \(1\). What fraction multiplies with \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) to equal \(1\)? Write your answer as \(a/b\).
hints: Multiplying fractions multiplies the tops together and the bottoms together. For the result to be \(1\), the top of the product and the bottom of the product have to come out equal. Picture what the second fraction's top and bottom would need to be so that the \(7\) and the \(12\) end up matched.

The \(7\) sits on top of \(\tfrac{7}{12}\), so to cancel it you need a \(7\) on the bottom of your fraction. The \(12\) sits on the bottom, so to cancel it you need a \(12\) on top. Put those together.
blocks.sol: You are hunting for a fraction \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) so that \(\tfrac{7}{12}\times\tfrac{a}{b}=1\). Multiplying fractions just multiplies tops and bottoms, which gives \(\tfrac{7\times a}{12\times b}\). A fraction equals \(1\) exactly when its top and bottom are the same number, so you want \(7\times a\) to match \(12\times b\) in the cleanest possible way. The simplest move is to make the top a \(12\) and the bottom a \(7\), because then the new top is \(7\times 12\) and the new bottom is \(12\times 7\), and those are the same value. So flipping \(\tfrac{7}{12}\) upside down hands you \(\tfrac{12}{7}\). Checking, \(\tfrac{7}{12}\times\tfrac{12}{7}=\tfrac{84}{84}=1\), which is the unchanged stick you wanted. The partner that reverses a fraction's scaling is always that fraction turned over, and it is called the reciprocal. \(\boxed{12/7}\)

blocks.x: The partner that multiplies with a fraction to land on exactly \(1\) is just that same fraction turned upside down, swap the top and the bottom and you have it, so the reciprocal of \(\tfrac{a}{b}\) is \(\tfrac{b}{a}\). The flip always works, because once you multiply you get \(a\) times \(b\) sitting over the very same \(a\) times \(b\), and any number over itself is \(1\). Three small things fall out of this. The number \(1\) flips to itself and so does \(-1\), and \(0\) has no reciprocal at all, because nothing you multiply by \(0\) can ever climb up to \(1\), which is the same reason you can never divide by \(0\).

blocks.cap: Flipping a fraction sends a point on one side of \(1\) to a partner on the other side, and the two always multiply to \(1\). Here \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) sits left of \(1\) and its reciprocal \(\tfrac{3}{2}\) sits right of \(1\), and \(\tfrac{3}{5}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{3}\) straddle \(1\) the same way. Only \(1\) sits on the pivot and partners with itself, while \(0\) has no partner because nothing reaches all the way up to \(1\) from \(0\).

blocks.x: A timber crew has a single straight plank that is \(3\) meters long. Five builders are working on the same wall, and they agree to split the plank into five equal pieces so each builder gets exactly the same length. Sharing equally among five people is what division by \(5\) means, so each builder's share is the plank \(\div 5\). Now look at that same share a second way. One equal piece out of five is one fifth of the whole plank, and back in Chapter 1 we learned that taking \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of something means multiplying by \(\tfrac{1}{5}\). So cutting the plank into \(5\) equal parts and keeping one part is the very same act as multiplying the length by \(\tfrac{1}{5}\). Using that idea, how long is each builder's share, in meters, written as a fraction \(a/b\)?
hints: Splitting the plank into five equal pieces and keeping one piece is exactly taking one fifth of the plank. So instead of \(3 \div 5\), you can ask what is \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of \(3\) meters.
Taking \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of something means multiplying by \(\tfrac{1}{5}\), so each share is \(3 \times \tfrac{1}{5}\). Multiply the \(3\) by the top of the fraction and keep the \(5\) on the bottom.
blocks.sol: Each builder gets the plank shared equally among five people, which is \(3 \div 5\). The cleanest way to see what that number is comes from the meaning of division itself. Splitting the plank into \(5\) equal pieces and keeping one piece is the same as taking one fifth of the plank, and taking \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of a length means multiplying that length by \(\tfrac{1}{5}\). So each share is $$3 \times \tfrac{1}{5}.$$ Picture three separate one meter rods laid end to end. If you cut every rod into five equal slivers, each sliver is \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) of a meter, and one builder collects one sliver from each of the three rods, which is three of those fifth meter slivers. Three copies of \(\tfrac{1}{5}\) is \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). This is the quiet truth hiding behind division by a whole number. Dividing \(3\) by \(5\) is multiplying \(3\) by the reciprocal \(\tfrac{1}{5}\), and the answer is simply the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{5}\). Each builder's share is \(\dfrac{3}{5}\) of a meter, so $$\boxed{3/5}.$$

blocks.x: Dividing by a number always asks the same thing, how many of it fit, and when the divisor is a fraction that count grows by the flip. Splitting something into \(b\) equal shares and keeping one is just taking \(\tfrac{1}{b}\) of it, so dividing by \(b\) and multiplying by \(\tfrac{1}{b}\) are the same move. And asking how many quarter cups fit in \(3\) cups gives \(12\), because each whole cup holds \(4\) of them, and that \(4\) is exactly the reciprocal of \(\tfrac{1}{4}\). Both pictures land in the same place, so to divide by any fraction I flip it to its reciprocal and multiply, handing every division straight back to the multiplying I already know.

A roll of decorative trim is \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of a meter long. You want to cut it into pieces that are each \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) of a meter long, and you are wondering how many of those pieces would fit. The "how many pieces fit" question is a division, so you set it up as \(\tfrac{5}{6} \div \tfrac{2}{9}\). This is the first time both fractions are real fractions, not just \(\tfrac{1}{\text{something}}\), so be careful which one you flip. Leave the first fraction alone and flip the divisor. What is \(\tfrac{5}{6} \div \tfrac{2}{9}\), written as a fraction \(a/b\) in lowest terms?
hints: Only the divisor flips. The \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) stays exactly as it is, and \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) turns into \(\tfrac{9}{2}\). Now the \(\div\) becomes a \(\times\), so you are really finding \(\tfrac{5}{6} \times \tfrac{9}{2}\).

Before you multiply across, look for a shared factor. The \(9\) on top and the \(6\) on the bottom both have a factor of \(3\), so the \(9\) becomes \(3\) and the \(6\) becomes \(2\). Then multiply straight across, \(5 \times 3\) over \(2 \times 2\).
blocks.sol: Asking how many \(\tfrac{2}{9}\)-meter pieces fit inside \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of a meter is a division question, \(\tfrac{5}{6} \div \tfrac{2}{9}\). To divide by a fraction, keep the first one and flip the second, then multiply. The \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) stays put and \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) flips to \(\tfrac{9}{2}\), so $$\frac{5}{6} \div \frac{2}{9} = \frac{5}{6} \times \frac{9}{2}.$$ Now cancel before multiplying. The \(9\) on top and the \(6\) on the bottom share a factor of \(3\), so the \(9\) shrinks to \(3\) and the \(6\) shrinks to \(2\). That leaves $$\frac{5}{6} \times \frac{9}{2} = \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{5 \times 3}{2 \times 2} = \frac{15}{4}.$$ So \(\tfrac{15}{4}\) of those pieces fit, which is \(3\tfrac{3}{4}\) pieces. You get three full pieces and three quarters of another one left over, which matches the "how many fit" feeling. The fully reduced answer is \(\boxed{15/4}\).
